title: The minimal example of quantum network Bell nonlocality
authors: Erwan Don, Jessica Bavaresco, Patryk Lipka-Bartosik, Nicolas Gisin, Nicolas Brunner, Alejandro Pozas-Kerstjens
abstract: In recent years, the study of Bell nonlocality has been generalized to quantum networks, where multiple independent sources distribute physical systems to distant parties who perform local measurements. In this context, a central open question is to identify the minimal network configuration in which quantum resources produce Bell nonlocal correlations. Here we address this question and show that quantum nonlocality is possible in the triangle network where the parties have no input choices and produce only binary-valued outcomes. To do so, we start by identifying a family of target distributions and proving their nonlocality. Next, we construct an explicit quantum model that reproduces the target distributions to machine precision. For this, we develop an efficient method for parameterizing quantum distributions in networks, inspired by the formalism of higher-order quantum operations. When considering the number of observed variables and their cardinality, this constitutes the smallest scenario possible that supports quantum nonlocality in networks. Moreover, by analyzing the explicit quantum model, we obtain new insights into how nonlocal distributions can be generated in quantum networks.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the simplest possible quantum network—a triangle of three parties with no input choices and binary outcomes—can exhibit Bell nonlocality. By developing an efficient parameterization method, the authors provide an explicit quantum model that achieves this, establishing a new minimal benchmark for quantum network nonlocality.
main_problem: Identifying the minimal network configuration capable of producing Bell nonlocality, specifically investigating if nonlocality can exist in a triangle network with no input choices and binary outcomes.
main_result: The authors prove that quantum Bell nonlocality is possible in the binary-outcome triangle network, establishing it as the smallest possible scenario for network nonlocality and disproving the conjecture that such a network must be local.
method: The study uses an efficient 'quantum tester' formalism based on higher-order quantum operations, a seesaw-type optimization algorithm for semidefinite programs (SDPs), and inflation methods to prove nonlocality.
model_or_system: A triangle network consisting of three parties (A, B, and C) connected by three bipartite sources. The model uses one entangled two-qubit source and two classical sources, where nonlocality is driven by the presence of a classical coin in the quantum source.
key_observables: Binary-valued outcomes (a, b, c) from local measurements.
important_parameters: Visibility parameter (v) of the target W-distribution, measurement angles, and the probability (p_phi) of the classical coin state.
main_assumptions: The set of local distributions in networks is non-convex, meaning nonlocality is formally proven only for specific discrete values of visibility rather than a continuous interval.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the triangle network topology and the transformation into independent testers; Fig 2 illustrates the quantum model architecture with classical coins and entangled states; Fig 3 plots model parameters against visibility; Fig 4 shows the Euclidean distance between the model and target distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of minimal network nonlocality, defines the triangle network and target distributions, presents a new quantum tester formalism for efficient optimization, demonstrates the construction of a nonlocal quantum model, and analyzes the visibility thresholds and parameter regimes.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it identifies the fundamental limits of nonlocality in networked architectures and introduces new mathematical tools for characterizing multipartite correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The binary-outcome triangle network. Parties A, B and C are pairwise connected by three bipartite sources that distribute quantum states ρα, ρβ and ργ. Each party performs a measurement on their respective subsystems, resulting in a binary-valued outcome, denoted a, b, c ∈{0, 1} respectively. We characterize the setup by the joint distribution of outcomes, p(a, b, c). (b) We combine each source with an adjacent measurement in order to characterize the quantum distribution in terms of three independent testers. This enables a more efficient and stable numerical exploration of the set of quantum distributions.

FIG. 2. The quantum model that reproduces the target dis- tributions Wv for visibilities up to v ≤0.6245 hence demon- strating nonlocality. The free parameters are the bias of the (pink) coins that connect to party C (the same for both), the bias of the coin that determines whether A and B measure the partially entangled, two-qubit quantum state |ω⟩, the param- eter that characterizes that state, and two more parameters determining the measurements of parties A and B in the X-Z plane. In the devices of A and B, the coin determines whether they output the coin values received from the other sources or they use them to measure |ω⟩and output the results.

FIG. 4. Euclidean distance between the distributions given by the parameters in Fig. 3 and the corresponding Wv. Panel (a) depicts the distance to models over the interval of visibilities v ∈[0, v4], where v4 = 0.6245. The distributions for which we search for quantum realizations are equally spaced in the interval, with the distance between two consecutive ones being 1249 1 998 000. Panel (b) is a zoom-in on the region v ∈[0.58, v4]. There, the red circles correspond to values of v for which the corresponding Wv is known to have a triangle-local model [28], and the four blue squares correspond to values of v1 to v4 which we prove to be nonlocal. FIG. 3. Values of the parameters of the quantum model that reproduces the target distribution Wv, as a function of the visibility v. The parameters p0, p∅and ω can be written in terms of the remaining two (θ0 and θ1) and the visibility v. The gray vertical line denotes the value after which the resulting distribution is nonlocal.
